does: This paper applies machine unlearning techniques to text-to-music generation models to remove the influence of specific copyrighted training data without degrading overall model performance. The authors evaluate existing unlearning methods on a pre-trained text-to-music baseline, examining how effectively these approaches can forget targeted datasets while preserving the model's ability to generate music across other domains. The work provides empirical analysis of unlearning's challenges when applied to music generation, including trade-offs between removing unwanted content and maintaining generation quality, establishing preliminary benchmarks for measuring unlearning success in this creative domain.
connects: This work bridges machine unlearning, an emerging area focused on selectively removing learned information from models, with music generation systems that have raised copyright and artist exploitation concerns. It responds to growing tensions between generative AI capabilities and creative intellectual property rights, building on recent unlearning research developed for image and language models. The paper connects to broader discussions around consent and attribution in AI training, particularly addressing how generative models can be made to opt-out from using specific artists or works, extending unlearning beyond privacy-focused applications toward creative rights management.
enables: This foundational work opens pathways for deploying music generation systems with built-in mechanisms for copyright holder consent and opt-out capabilities. It could enable future systems where artists can request removal of their works from active models, supporting ethically responsible deployment of generative AI in creative industries. The analysis of unlearning efficacy in music may guide development of more efficient removal techniques and establish evaluation protocols for verifying successful unlearning. This could support regulatory compliance and licensing frameworks, ultimately making generative music models more compatible with existing creative ecosystems and intellectual property protections.
